text:
Iron (Fe) is an essential element for life on Earth and iron homeostasis in the human body is a critical process where iron must be sufficient for required processes but insufficient for non-required processes. In fact, other essential and non-essential elements including calcium (Ca), magnesium (Mg) and trace metals such as copper (Cu), zinc (Zn), lead (Pb), cadmium (Cd), mercury (Hg) and nickel (Ni) share many common proteins for their intestinal absorption and intracellular transport. For example, Cu2+ and Cd2+ inhibit iron absorption while high iron levels inhibit copper metabolism and decrease ceruloplasmin. High iron levels also inhibit zinc intestinal uptake. Transferrin is also a common carrier for zinc and nickel.

Calcium in a dose dependent manner inhibits divalent metal transporter 1 (DMT1) and thereby affects iron absorption. Low magnesium is a contributing factor to iron deficiency. The toxic metals lead, cadmium, mercury and nickel all impair iron homeostasis. In more detail, lead affects iron distribution and increased cadmium levels reduce iron uptake. Mercury is often found in elevated ferritin levels in the blood. Nickel has an impact on the cellular iron metabolism. Lead and cadmium levels increase in the blood of haemochromatosis patients undergoing phlebotomy to reduce iron levels, and similar changes in trace element levels have been observed in different forms of anaemia. The chronic effects of their interplay are not yet fully understood.
headline: Fully Human Written
prediction: We believe that this document is fully human-written
